THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. THE MOTION IS GRANTED. THIRD PARTY PG&E TO PRODUCE ALL RESPONSIVE DOCUMENTS WITHIN SEVEN DAYS. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Production From Third-Party Pacific Gas And Electric Company
Off Calendar Mar-22-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. ANTHONY S. CHALIFOUX, Esq. of Balestreri Potocki & Holmes (Tel: 619-686-1930) for defendant Advance Fiber Works, Inc.;
Atty. GAYLE L. GOUGH, Esq. of Gough & Hancock LLP (Tel: 415-848-8918) for nonparty Pacific Gas and Electric Company;
Atty. DANIEL R. BALTES, Esq. of Hugo Parker LLP (Tel: 415-808-0300) for defendants Mastec Services Company, Inc. and Mastec Renewables Construction Company, Inc.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT ADVANCED FIBER WORKS, INC's Motion To Compel Further Production From Third-Party Pacific Gas And Electric Company.

The parties to the motion stipulate to the matter being decided by the Judge Pro Tem (the Mastec defendants are not parties to the motion). The Judge Pro Tem and counsel for the parties to the motion authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. The motion is GRANTED. Third party PG&E to produce all responsive documents within seven days.

The prevailing party is to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and submit it by email to the Judge Pro Tem at noah@ndllegal.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Noah Lebowitz; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Peggy Tsujimoto, CSR No. 5229, Tel: 415-828-1919, Email: peggytsujimoto@gmail.com (302/JPT)

Off Calendar Jan-13-2020 Continued To Mar-11-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-13-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. ARA JABAGCHOURIAN, ESQ. of Law Offices of Ara Jabagchourian, P.C. (Tel: 650-437-6840) appearing for plaintiff;
Atty. ANOUSH C HOLADAY, ESQ. of Wood Smith Henning & Berman (Tel: 925-222-3400) appearing via CourtCall for defendant Kilford Engineering, Inc.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT KILFORD ENGINEERING, INC.'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Kilford Engineering, Inc.'s motion to strike is denied in its entirety. Defendant Kilford's request for judicial notice is denied.

In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.)

Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.)

Plaintiff alleges Defendant was reckless and in willful violation of California law when it ruptured a gas line. (Compl. 1-2.) As a result of Defendant's conduct, the pressure knocked Plaintiff to the ground causing injuries. (Id. 2, 14.) The gas eventually ignited causing an explosion and thereafter a fire which burned down five buildings. (Id. 1, 14.) Paragraph 24 of the Complaint sufficiently alleges a claim for punitive damages. Plaintiff alleges Defendant's conduct was despicable because it knowingly violated safety regulations while knowing of the associated risks. (Id. 24 ["Kilford's supervising agents and managing directors knew or should have known about these violations, yet failed to implement required regulations?"].)

The motion to strike paragraph 21 and 24 of the Complaint is denied. Plaintiff properly alleges that Defendant violated Cal-OSHA regulations as a basis for its negligence per se cause of action. (Evid. Code 669; Lab. Code 6304.5; Elsner v. Uveges (2004) 34 Cal.4th 915, 928, 935-936 ["In general, plaintiffs may use Cal-OSHA provisions to show a duty or standard of care to the same extent as any other regulation or statute, whether the defendant is their employer or a third party."].) Defendant cites no authority for the proposition that a plaintiff is precluded from alleging such a claim merely because the defendant denies the violations or they have not been finally adjudicated.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano, CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
